# From Architecture Question to Decision {#sec-architecture-20-ontology}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"All models are wrong, but some are useful."*
>
> — George E. P. Box, *Robustness in the Strategy of Scientific Model Building* (1979)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** George E. P. Box's point applies to more than prediction
models. A task description, benchmark, simulator, power model, or test suite
always leaves something out. Each can still be useful when its scope and
failure modes are clear and when the decision does not reach beyond the
evidence it provides.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This chapter follows a piece of AI-assisted architecture work from a broad goal to a specific comparison, evaluation with architecture tools, evidence, and the next decision. Along the way, it identifies what an AI system must be able to inspect, change, predict, and test, and what still requires an architect's judgment or a decision owner's approval.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What must architects make explicit so an AI system can work on the intended
problem, use engineering tools correctly, and produce results another architect
can evaluate before deciding what to do next?
:::

AI assistance becomes reviewable only when the path from an architecture
question to a decision is explicit. The architect must bound the task, identify
the legal design choices, expose the relevant design state, name the tools and
checks, state the claim under review, and assign authority for the next action.
Those elements keep an AI method, the tool path, and the reviewer focused on the
same comparison.

Give an AI system only a broad product goal and it may suggest plausible cache,
vector, or accelerator ideas. It cannot reliably know which comparison the
architect intends or which decision the results should support. If the
immediate task is to compare cache configurations, the system needs the
baseline, allowed cache choices, performance and power limits, simulator
settings, and evaluation budget. The architect must also state what would rule
out a candidate and who may act on the results. If any of this remains
unstated, the system can carry out a coherent comparison that is different from
the one the architect intended.

Architecture already manages complexity by writing down the relationships that
matter. Architects use models, simulators, workload characterizations, cost
estimates, design rules, and reviews to reason about large design spaces
[@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. AI adds a new kind of contributor
to that work, and one that can produce plausible errors that pass through
several tools before anyone notices them. Model choice therefore comes after
the architecture task and comparison are clear. The system needs to know what
it may see and change, how it obtains feedback, how its explanation will be
tested, what can count as evidence, what rules a result out, and who may decide
what happens next.

Each challenge points to information that architects must expose to an AI
method and preserve for review (@tbl-bounds-to-framework).

| **Architecture challenge** | **What must become explicit** |
| --- | --- |
| Missing or stale information | Representations record the workload, design, tool, and version state available to AI methods and reviewers. Prediction models state which consequences they can estimate and under what conditions. |
| Invalid or inadequate choices | The legal design space and tool environments define which changes are valid and which tool paths can run them. |
| Expensive or inconclusive evaluation | Evaluation budgets, fidelity rules, and evidence records show what the available feedback can support. |
| More results than reviewers can settle | Rejection conditions and checks show when a candidate must stop, and the authority assignment names who decides. |
| No clear next decision | The recorded evidence limits the conclusion, and the named decision owner decides what action, if any, follows. |

: **From architecture challenges to required information.** Each row connects a
failure mode to information that architects must make explicit before an AI
system can help with the comparison.
{#tbl-bounds-to-framework tbl-colwidths="[38,62]"}

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can describe the structure of an AI-assisted architecture
study, explain what an AI method can and cannot do inside it, and determine what
must remain with an architect or decision owner. You can:

- **Bound:** State the study's workload, baseline, design space, constraints, non-goals, and intended decision.
- **Distinguish:** Separate the study, its artifacts, its architectural claim, and the design loop used when work iterates.
- **Connect:** Relate represented state and legal actions to tools, AI jobs, feedback, and evidence.
- **Assign:** Separate the authority to propose, execute, reject, waive, recommend, and commit.
- **Use:** Apply the ontology and design-loop card without treating either as proof of architectural quality.
:::

## The Architecture 2.0 Ontology {#sec-arch2-ontology}

Here, *ontology* means a conceptual map of the objects and relationships in a
bounded AI-assisted architecture study. It connects the study's intent, task,
representations, allowed actions, tools, AI jobs, feedback, evidence,
claim, and decision authority. It is not a formal machine-interpretable
ontology, and completing it does not establish that a design is sound.

Above RTL, architects often lack a shared record that connects the original
question to experiments, evidence, and decisions across tools. From RTL down,
design flows already use artifacts that tools can parse, transform, reject, and
sign off. High-level synthesis, modeling languages, compiler IRs, and EDA
frameworks also structure parts of the path to implementation. Yet much of the
reasoning about *what* to build remains scattered across whiteboards,
spreadsheets, scripts, slides, meeting notes, and natural-language
specifications.

@fig-structured-layer-above-rtl places a bounded architecture study between the
architect's question and the tools used to investigate it. The figure's middle
layer is the bounded comparison and its review record. It connects reasoning
that often stays in architects' heads or working notes to executable
implementation flows, giving tools, AI systems, reviewers, and architects a
shared question for interpreting each artifact and tool result.

![**An AI-assisted architecture study connects intent to implementation evidence.** The bounded study contains the scope, architectural claim, execution and analysis process, and evidence needed for review. Iterative work uses a design loop.](images/F4d-structured-layer-above-rtl){#fig-structured-layer-above-rtl width="100%" fig-alt="Three-layer diagram showing architecture intent and judgment flowing into a structured architecture study, with tool results flowing back for evidence review."}

Read @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl from top to bottom. The upper layer contains
the design intent, architecture models, requirements, and rationale that shape
the architect's question. The middle layer turns that intent into a bounded
comparison. Its scope names the question and intended decision, the
architectural claim says what will be reviewed, execution and analysis produce
results, and a reviewer judges what the recorded evidence supports.

The lower layer is the executable tool path. Simulators and analytic models,
compilers and runtimes, RTL generators, and EDA flows carry out different parts
of the comparison. Their measurements enter the evidence record, and a reviewer
assesses whether they support the claim. The named decision owner may accept a
finding limited to the stated scope, authorize the next step, or send the work
back to revise the claim or scope. One person may hold both roles, but reviewing
the evidence and authorizing action are separate rights.

The middle layer is therefore not another architecture tool. It is the explicit
connection between design intent and tool execution, and between tool results
and the next architectural decision. Coding-agent systems provide a familiar
example of a similar connection. A repository-level coding agent receives a
bounded issue, a known repository state, permitted editing and command-line
actions, executable checks, and a patch that another developer can review. The
model works inside that structure rather than deciding for itself what software
problem should be solved.

```{=latex}
\Needspace{32\baselineskip}
```

::: {.callout-field-note title="What Coding-Agent Benchmarks Made Explicit"}
SWE-bench turns repository-level coding into a bounded task. Each problem starts
with an issue description and a fixed repository state. An agent edits the
repository, and hidden tests check whether the issue was fixed without breaking
existing behavior [@JimenezEtAl2024SWEBench]. This is more useful than testing
code generation in isolation because it connects an issue, working state,
allowed changes, executable checks, and a reviewable result.

SWE-agent and OpenHands make the interface around the model explicit. They let
an agent inspect files, edit code, use a command line, and run programs inside a
controlled environment [@YangEtAl2024SWEAgent; @WangEtAl2024OpenHands]. The
model is only one part of the system. Its state, actions, and feedback also
shape the result.

The benchmarks have limits. SWE-bench Verified used human review and
containerized environments to remove underspecified tasks, faulty tests, and
environment failures. A later audit still found flawed tests and training
exposure that weakened the score as a measure of current coding capability
[@OpenAI2026SWEBenchLimits]. Terminal-Bench 2.0 uses the same general pattern
for terminal tasks by pairing each task with an environment, a human-written
solution, and verification tests [@MerrillEtAl2026TerminalBench].
:::

The shared structure connects a task to working state, allowed actions, tool
feedback, and review. Architecture needs that connection without pretending
that the domains are the same. A software issue or terminal task can often end
with tests of the final state. No single architecture test usually settles
performance, power, physical feasibility, software support, and product risk.

Architecture state is also spread across specifications, traces, simulator
settings, compiler paths, RTL, physical-design reports, and earlier decisions.
The relevant tools return feedback with different costs and fidelity, and
reviewers must decide what can serve as evidence. Some decisions are also
expensive to reverse. An AI-assisted architecture study therefore needs the
explicit task, state, actions, tools, and checks seen in coding-agent
evaluations, together with a bounded architectural claim, evidence at the right
fidelity, and a named decision owner.
@fig-structured-layer-above-rtl connects that recorded task and working state to
the tool path, evidence, and the next decision.

The terms inside the middle layer refer to different things. The bounded
architecture study is the unit of work, and it may produce understanding,
artifacts, findings, or decisions. An artifact is an implementation or proposal
produced by that work. The architectural claim is what another architect
evaluates. When the work iterates, the process used to execute and analyze it is
a design loop. The design-loop card introduced near the end provides an index
into the underlying records.

@fig-ontology-chain shows the parts inside the bounded study introduced in
@fig-structured-layer-above-rtl. It traces that work from a scoped question to
the next decision. The recorded scope and claim specify the comparison. The
representation and tool interfaces show architects and AI methods which actions
are available. A reviewer examines the recorded results against the stated
checks, and the named decision owner judges what conclusion the evidence
supports and who may act next.

![**The ontology connects a scoped study to an architectural decision.** Architects establish the scope and state the claim for review, while representations and environments make proposed actions executable. When work iterates, a design loop organizes execution and analysis. A reviewer examines the evidence, and the decision owner may accept a finding, narrow the claim, or revise the study.](images/F4-ontology-chain){#fig-ontology-chain width="100%" fig-alt="An ontology connecting a scoped architecture study and its architectural claim to representations, tools, an execution and analysis process, evidence, review, a named decision owner, and a scoped finding or decision."}

@fig-structured-layer-above-rtl locates the bounded study between architectural
judgment and the tool path. @fig-ontology-chain opens that middle layer. The
architect states the comparison and intended decision in the scope, then frames
the statement that another architect must review as a claim. Representations,
tools, and the allowed design space limit what architects and AI methods can
execute. The execution record shows which AI job was performed, which tool produced
each result, and how the result was checked. A reviewer examines the resulting
evidence against the claim, and the named decision owner decides whether to
accept a finding or authorize the next step. The red return path shows that a
failed check may require more than another candidate. It may expose a problem in
the claim or the original scope.

The boxes in @fig-ontology-chain should look familiar because architects already
do much of this work manually. AI does not require a new design process. It
requires a clearer record of the process architects already use. An architect
may fill in a missing constraint from experience or remember why an earlier
design was rejected. An AI method cannot reliably use that project-specific
context unless the architect writes it down. The method needs access to the
scope, constraints, prior decisions, and evidence. Tool interfaces must expose
legal actions and useful results, and the architect must name who may reject a
candidate or authorize the next step.

## From Product Intent to a Bounded Study

In @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl, the architecture question and the judgment
behind it establish the starting intent. The lighthouse prompt states that
intent.

> *Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an
> XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a vector-capable CPU,
> accelerator, or SoC block under a 3 W TDP target in a 3 nm-class low-power
> mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected
> alternatives.*

The product intent is to improve mobile XR efficiency. It is not yet an
architecture task. The next piece of work might characterize the workload,
compare cache organizations, estimate memory traffic, or prepare a design-space
report. An AI system given only the product intent cannot know which of these
comparisons the architect needs or what decision should follow. Once the
architect chooses the next task, that choice determines which information,
tools, and evidence are needed, what can be measured, how costly an error would
be, and how much freedom an AI method should have.

> **Bounded architecture study.** A bounded architecture study turns a broad
> goal into a specific piece of architecture work. It states the question,
> workload or scenario, baseline, allowed design choices, objectives,
> constraints, tools, planned evidence, and decision the work is meant to
> inform. It also says what the study will not try to decide.

One bounded study could compare a defined set of cache organizations for named
XRBench traces against a stated RISC-V baseline under the 3\ W power envelope,
using an agreed simulator and power model. Its immediate purpose might be to
decide which cache organization, if any, merits more detailed RTL work. It
would not claim to have designed the complete XR subsystem or approved a
product configuration. This is the bounded study scope in
@fig-ontology-chain.

That comparison, its limits, and its intended decision define the bounded
study. Simulator runs, generated cache configurations, plots, and reports are
artifacts or recorded results within it. The design loop is the repeated work
used to produce and assess them. The work may lead to better understanding, an
implementation, a map of the design space, a finding, or a decision, but another
architect should always be able to recover what was compared and what the
evidence actually supports.

Critiquing a report, predicting energy, searching an accelerator tiling space,
and editing RTL each require different information, tools, and checks. The
task has to be clear before the method is chosen. Only then does it make sense
to decide whether the method should generate, predict, optimize, critique,
repair, verify, explain, or coordinate.

Bounded does not mean permanently fixed. An architect may discover that another
workload or memory organization matters and expand the work. When the question
changes, the architect must update the recorded scope rather than quietly treat
evidence from the original comparison as if it covered the larger question.

## The Architectural Claim Defines What Gets Reviewed

In @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl and @fig-ontology-chain, the bounded scope
leads to an architectural claim. Stating the claim lets architects distinguish
an artifact from the statement they intend to test. Asking whether an AI system
can design hardware is too broad to guide an architecture review. A more useful
claim says what the comparison is meant to show, where that statement would
apply, and what evidence could support or challenge it. Architects rarely
accept an artifact in isolation. They judge what it shows for a stated
workload, baseline, design space, objectives, and constraints. The statement
they evaluate is the architectural claim.

> **Architectural claim.** An architectural claim says what result the architect
> expects from a proposed change, why that result should occur, and where it
> should hold. It identifies the proposed mechanism, workload or scenario,
> baseline, allowed design choices, objectives, constraints, and evidence.
> Before evaluation, it also identifies the evidence that would count against
> the claim. After evaluation, the architect updates it if needed to match what
> the evidence supports and states what remains unestablished.

In the lighthouse cache comparison, a claim might say that one cache
organization reduces simulated memory stalls by lowering conflict misses for
the named XRBench traces relative to the stated baseline while remaining inside
the modeled 3\ W envelope. The claim would be limited to those traces,
simulator settings, and power assumptions. Those limits define what the result
can show. It would not yet establish post-layout power, timing closure, or
product readiness.

> **What can rule a candidate out?** Before running the comparison, the
> architect should state which result would rule out a candidate or narrow the
> claim, how that result will be checked, and who may stop or redirect the work.
> An automated check may do so only under an agreed policy. The named decision
> owner remains responsible for exceptions, waivers, and decisions with larger
> consequences.

For this comparison, the stated rules might reject any configuration that
exceeds the 3\ W envelope under the agreed power model. The power-model run is
the check. A script may flag the candidate automatically, but the architect
still decides whether to drop the candidate, investigate the model, or send an
exception for review.

> **What next action does the evidence support?** The decision owner should
> state the strongest next action the current evidence supports. That may be
> much narrower than the decisions that could eventually follow.

For the same cache result, an early estimate may justify keeping the candidate
in the search but nothing more. Cycle-level simulation and a calibrated power
model may justify selecting it for RTL evaluation, but not choosing a product
configuration. Post-layout or silicon evidence may later justify a step that is
harder to reverse.

The limit on the claim and the limit on the next action answer different
questions. The first says what the result shows. The second says what the
decision owner will authorize on the strength of that result. As a decision
becomes harder to reverse or more consequential, it needs stronger evidence
and more independent challenge. The owner remains responsible for any action
that goes beyond what the evidence alone supports.

Framing the claim before running the evaluation matters because the same tool
result can mean different things under different workloads, baselines, or
objectives. In the claim, the architect records the comparison, the evidence
that could support it, and the results that would rule out a candidate. After
the evaluation, the architect confirms or narrows the claim to match the
evidence and states which next decision the current evidence can support.
Without that context, a generated design may be technically plausible without
answering the architecture question.

## The Design Space Defines What AI May Change

An architectural claim is meaningful only relative to a design space. A system
that reports "the best" candidate without exposing the alternatives, invalid
regions, baseline, and tradeoffs has hidden the comparison that gives the
result meaning.

In architecture, the design space is not everything a model can generate. It is
the set of choices allowed by the task and its architecture constraints. A
method may be able to produce many more configurations, but some will violate
ISA compatibility, memory semantics, software support, timing assumptions,
power limits, package constraints, verification requirements, or deployment
policy.

The current toolchain may support only part of the legal design space. A
candidate can be valid for the architecture task but impossible to evaluate
with the available tools. The architect should set it aside or find another way
to evaluate it rather than label it invalid. A candidate outside the legal
design space does not belong in the current comparison unless the architect
revises the scope or constraints and records why. In
@fig-structured-layer-above-rtl, the design-space rules connect the declared
scope to the tool path and execution process.

In the cache comparison, the allowed changes might be cache capacity,
associativity, and replacement policy. The RISC-V baseline, XRBench traces, and
3\ W limit remain fixed. Some cache configurations are legal but unattractive.
Some look promising under a proxy but fail in simulation or under the power
model. Others use unsupported parameters and should never consume a full tool
run. If none of the allowed choices can meet the stated requirements, the AI
system should report that result rather than quietly change the workload or
power limit.

Architects usually have to weigh several objectives at once, including
performance, energy, latency, area, reliability, programmability, verification
effort, and cost. A design-space report should document the alternatives
explored, the tradeoffs observed, the candidates rejected, and the decisions
that remain.

## Representations and Prediction Models

AI methods and the tools in @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl can act only on
information architects make available. The chosen representation determines
what architects, reviewers, and AI systems can inspect and use. Architecture
knowledge lives in many forms, including natural-language specifications, ISA
documents, traces, graphs, simulator configurations, RTL, compiler IR, EDA
reports, benchmark metadata, scripts, and review notes. An architect reading a
cache spreadsheet may know that a capacity is per core, remember that the line
size is fixed, or recall that a similar configuration already failed timing. An
AI method does not infer those assumptions from the spreadsheet alone. The
architect must write them down. Hidden defaults, undocumented constraints, and
forgotten rejections can produce an invalid or misleading result even when the
proposed artifact looks plausible.

For the cache comparison, the representation would include the baseline
configuration, legal cache parameters, workload traces, simulator and power
model versions, constraints, and results from earlier runs. If the AI method
sees miss rates but not the 3\ W constraint, it may propose a configuration that
looks faster but cannot satisfy the stated power requirement. The missing
information, rather than the search method, is then the immediate problem.

The representation records the current workload, design state, assumptions,
and earlier results. A prediction model estimates what may happen after
something changes. In the cache example, it might estimate how greater
associativity would affect misses, cycles, and power before a costly full
simulation. The prediction may come from a simulator, learned surrogate,
calibrated equation, or design rule. Its usefulness is limited to the workloads,
design ranges, and tool conditions for which architects have checked its
accuracy.

AI methods often use such models to estimate how represented state may change
after an action [@HaSchmidhuber2018WorldModels;
@SuttonBarto2018ReinforcementLearning]. The model does not need to cover the
entire processor or system. It needs to predict the consequences that matter for
the allowed change and the current task. Its prediction is useful only within
the conditions and fidelity for which it has been checked.

## Tool Interfaces for AI-Assisted Architecture Work

Representing the relevant information is only the first step. An AI method can
use the tools shown in @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl only when their
interfaces make the allowed actions and returned results clear.

A tool environment combines an architecture tool with an interface that defines
how an AI system may use it. The interface specifies legal actions, available
observations, execution cost, provenance, and failure behavior.
@fig-tools-become-environments contrasts such an environment with a basic
wrapper that only submits a command and returns a result.

For the cache comparison, the interface might allow changes to cache size,
associativity, and replacement policy, then return cycles, misses, estimated
power, run time, and failure messages. It would reject unsupported values and
record the simulator and model versions used for each run. A command wrapper
that returns only a log file would leave much of this behavior implicit.

![**Information required for an executable tool environment.** Legal actions, observations, costs, fidelity, provenance, handling of invalid actions, and failure signals let a reviewer trace tool feedback. The environment does not determine whether that feedback is sufficient evidence or decide the architectural claim.](images/F4e-tools-become-environments){#fig-tools-become-environments width="100%" fig-alt="Side-by-side diagram contrasting a basic tool wrapper with an architecture environment that specifies legal actions, observations, cost and fidelity, provenance, handling of invalid actions, and failure signals."}

@fig-tools-become-environments shows why a command wrapper and an environment
are not equivalent. Both may run the same simulator, but the wrapper can leave
legal parameter ranges, failure handling, evaluation cost, and provenance
implicit. The environment exposes those conditions so AI methods operate under
the same rules and reviewers can interpret the returned measurements.

ArchGym, an open-source evaluation environment, is an important example because
it treats the connection between search algorithms and architecture simulators
as a first-class interface [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. Its relevance here is not
any one search algorithm. It shows why a simulator interface must specify legal
actions, what happens after an invalid action, feedback cost, provenance,
comparable baselines, and failure signals. These properties must remain visible
when the interface changes, because a wrapper revision can change which actions
are legal or how a failure is interpreted even when the underlying simulator
does not change.

## The Design Loop Makes AI Participation Inspectable

The execution-and-analysis box in @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl and
@fig-ontology-chain shows where an AI method acts on the recorded comparison.
Architects already move between a design, a tool result, and the next change.
That loop is not new. What changes is that an AI system cannot reliably recover
the assumptions an architect carries from one tool or meeting to the next. It
needs the current design state, permitted changes, tool interfaces, stopping
rules, and review points to be recorded alongside the work.

> **Architecture design loop.** An architecture design loop is the repeated
> process of examining the current design, making an allowed change, running the
> relevant tools, interpreting the result, and deciding whether to revise,
> reject, or continue. Each iteration retains enough information for another
> architect to see what led to the next step.

The coding-agent example gives this loop a familiar shape. Repository files and
version history provide visible state. Editing and command-line tools define
available actions. Tests, compilers, and program output return feedback. The
proposed change remains available for review. An architecture loop needs the
same connections, but its state crosses more kinds of artifacts and its checks
do not reduce to one pass-or-fail test suite.

The difficult part is keeping the comparison intact as work crosses tools. A
simulator may report fewer cycles while a power model reports that the same
candidate exceeds the power limit. That result may trigger an automated
rejection rule or lead the architect to reject the candidate. A compiler change
may improve one workload and invalidate another. The AI system must receive both
results without silently changing the workload, baseline, or constraints. The
record must also preserve who or what made each change and why the next action
followed.

Return to the bounded cache comparison. One iteration begins with the baseline
cache configuration, the allowed changes, and the named XRBench traces. An AI
method proposes a legal configuration, and the simulator measures its
performance. The power model may then show that the candidate exceeds the 3\ W
envelope. If so, an automated check may remove it from consideration under the
recorded rejection rule. If it survives, the architect records the result and
decides whether another configuration is worth trying or whether the evidence
is ready for review.

@fig-design-loop expands the execution-and-analysis box into one iteration. An
architect or AI method reads the recorded state, makes an allowed change, and
runs the relevant tool. An automated check may reject or escalate a candidate
when a declared check fails. A reviewer assesses the evidence and records what
it supports. The decision owner then decides whether to accept or narrow the
claim, reject the candidate, or request another iteration. Objectives and
constraints limit the work, while stopping and escalation rules determine
whether another iteration is worthwhile.

![**How an iterative architecture study proceeds.** An architect or AI method reads the recorded design state, makes an allowed change, runs a tool, and submits the result for review. The reviewer records what the evidence supports, and the decision owner determines what happens next. A higher-fidelity result does not by itself authorize a more consequential decision.](images/F3-design-loop){#fig-design-loop width="100%" fig-alt="A design loop within a scoped architecture study. Represented state, permitted action, tool feedback, and evidence lead to claim review. A scoped finding or decision can leave the loop, while revision updates the recorded state for another turn."}

The bounded scope tells architects and AI methods what work is in bounds. The
claim tells reviewers which conclusion to assess, and the design-loop record
shows how the candidates and evidence developed. An AI model contributes inside
that process. It may generate candidates, summarize evidence, predict outcomes,
call tools, critique assumptions, or coordinate subtasks. The declared
comparison, experiments, and review determine whether those outputs support the
claim.

Inside that loop, the word "agent" says little about what an AI system actually
does. A generator proposes candidates, a predictor estimates behavior before
an expensive evaluation, and an optimizer chooses what to try next. Critics
and verifiers challenge assumptions and check constraints. Other roles may
repair an artifact that fails a check, explain a tradeoff, or coordinate tool
calls. A reviewer needs to know which role each method played, what information
it received, and what it returned.

In the cache comparison, a generator could propose legal configurations, a
predictor could screen configurations against performance and power estimates,
and an optimizer could choose which one to simulate next. The simulator and
power model would test the candidate, and the agreed checks would determine
whether it remains under consideration. The architect would decide whether the
evidence is strong enough to begin RTL work. Naming the method alone would
explain none of this.

Language models, search procedures, learned surrogates, scripts, formal tools,
architects, and tool engineers can perform these roles in different
combinations. A reviewer should be able to tell who or what performed each role,
what information it used, what it changed, and how the result was checked. The
composition matters more than the label attached to the overall system.

The roles assigned to an AI system do not grant decision authority. A named
decision owner or engineering organization still takes responsibility for the
objectives, exceptions, and major decisions. The owner does not need to approve
every configuration or tool call.

## Feedback Becomes Evidence

In @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl, tool measurements feed into evidence and
review. When an AI method changes an allowed parameter and a tool completes the
run, the result is feedback. It does not automatically count as evidence. A
simulator result, benchmark score, synthesis report, generated explanation, or
model confidence value is feedback. A reviewer can treat it as evidence only
when it is tied to a particular claim and the run record supplies the
conditions under which it was produced and its provenance. The designated
reviewer must still judge what the result means for the comparison.

The strength of the check must match the next decision. A proxy may be enough
to keep a candidate in the search, while starting RTL work or making a product
decision requires stronger evidence from tools closer to the implemented
system.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="A 3 W claim needs evidence, not a number"}
**Context.** A reported power result is feedback. It becomes evidence only when
the run record provides enough information for a reviewer to judge what the
number means.

**In the cache comparison.** A statement that one cache configuration remains
inside the modeled 3\ W envelope needs the named XRBench traces, RISC-V
baseline, cache parameters, simulator and power-model versions, relevant
software assumptions, uncertainty, and the check that would rule the
configuration out.

**Evidence rule.** The same power number supports different decisions depending
on whether it came from a proxy, cycle-level simulation, synthesis, a
post-layout estimate, or silicon. A proxy may be enough to keep a candidate in
consideration. Stronger implementation or deployment decisions require evidence
closer to the final system.

The design-space report must say what decision the current evidence supports and
which later decisions still require stronger evidence.
:::

Negative results can also serve as evidence. Rejected candidates, failed
simulator runs, invalid configurations, proxy wins that disappear at higher
fidelity, and abandoned assumptions help map the design space. They show why a
candidate that passed the checks deserves attention and what could overturn the
claim. Preserving these results also prevents a later method or reviewer from
repeating an already-discredited path without realizing it.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Feedback and Evidence Check"}
Before advancing a model-generated candidate, ask where the evidence came from,
what result would rule the candidate out, how that result will be checked, who
may stop the work, and who makes the final decision. If those questions have no
recorded answers, the evidence is not yet strong enough for the next step.
:::

## Authority Depends on the Action

In @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl, a reviewer examines the evidence before the
named decision owner accepts a finding within the stated scope or approves the
next architectural step. Describing a system as autonomous does not specify
what it is allowed to do. Authority belongs to particular actions; it does not
come from the model's capability. Architects should assign separate decision
rights instead of giving the system one overall level of automation
(@tbl-decision-rights-matrix).

| **Right** | **What it authorizes** | **Required record** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Propose** | Produce a candidate, hypothesis, test, or change for review. | Person or system, allowed output type, scope, budget, and required provenance. |
| **Execute** | Run a declared action inside the environment. | Legal actions, resource limits, stop conditions, failure handling, and rollback path. |
| **Reject** | Block a candidate, claim, or next step when a named rejection check fails. | Check, threshold, evidence source, whether the check is independent, and who may reject. |
| **Waive** | Permit a failed or unmet rejection check to be bypassed under stated conditions. | Waiver holder, rationale, expiration, residual risk, and reopening condition. |
| **Recommend** | Summarize the evidence and recommend a specific next step. | Recommender, supported scope, alternatives, what the recommendation does not cover, and uncertainty. |
| **Commit** | Approve the next high-impact step and accept its remaining risk. | Decision owner, approved step, accepted evidence, and conditions for reversal or revalidation. |

: **Decision rights are independent assignments.** A recorded policy may
authorize an AI system to propose, execute, reject under a stated check, or
recommend. Waive and commit rights remain with named people or organizations.
For each right, the assignment should state its scope, required evidence, and
consequences. A model's technical capability does not by itself grant authority.
{#tbl-decision-rights-matrix tbl-colwidths="[14,34,42]"}

The separation appears in ordinary workflows. A model may propose a cache
configuration, a harness may execute it, and an automated check may reject it
when it fails the agreed threshold. A review lead may decide whether to allow an
exception, an architect may recommend a tradeoff, and a product lead may decide
whether to commit resources to RTL implementation. Several rights may belong to
the same person or organization, but holding one does not automatically grant
another.

The named architect or engineering organization remains responsible for framing
the work and authorizing major next steps. This does not limit the work an AI
method may perform. It identifies who must answer for the comparison, the
evidence, and the consequences. The recorded assignments show who holds each
right, but they cannot prove that the reviewer is sufficiently independent or
that the evidence is strong enough.

How much authority to grant depends on how hard an action is to reverse and
what happens if it is wrong. A compiler flag can often be rolled back after
deployment telemetry, while an RTL change may affect timing closure and a
mask-level choice may be irreversible. As the cost of reversal rises, the
decision needs stronger evidence, more independent challenge, and a named
decision owner. Feedback cost, reversibility, and consequence therefore belong
in the authority assignment rather than being inferred from the model's
capability.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint}
Before assigning execute, reject, waive, recommend, or commit rights, ask what
actions are allowed, what evidence is required, when the work must be escalated,
who owns the decision, and what happens if the decision is wrong. If the
assignment does not answer those questions, do not grant the right yet.
:::

## Reviewing the Work in Three Passes

The scope, claim, represented state, tools, evidence, and authority assignments
give a reader a practical way to inspect AI-assisted architecture work. Follow
@fig-structured-layer-above-rtl in three passes. The first checks the intended
comparison. The second checks what the architects and AI methods could see,
change, and evaluate. The third asks what the evidence supports and what the
decision owner may authorize next. These are reading passes, not fixed stages
in the work.

An early demonstration may not have every answer. It can still be useful, but
its claim must remain narrow enough to match the information and evidence that
are available. The reader should be able to see what remains unknown and which
decisions the demonstration cannot yet support.

@tbl-compact-framework-checklist applies the three passes to the running cache
example.

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

| **Reading pass** | **Reader question** | **Cache comparison** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Scope** | What is the comparison meant to show, for which workload and baseline, and among which design choices? | Compare a defined set of cache organizations for named XRBench traces against a stated RISC-V baseline under the 3\ W envelope, then decide whether one merits RTL evaluation. |
| **Execution** | What information can the AI methods use, what may they change, which roles do they play, and which tools and evaluation budget are available? | Workload traces, legal cache parameters, compiler assumptions, a simulator and power model, candidate generation or search, and a declared simulation budget. |
| **Judgment** | Which results support or challenge the finding, why did the result occur, and what may the decision owner authorize next? | Performance and power results, rejected configurations, sensitivity analysis, an explanation based on misses and memory traffic, and a decision about further RTL work. |

: **Three passes through an architecture study.** Scope, execution, and judgment each pair the reader's question with the running lighthouse cache comparison. {#tbl-compact-framework-checklist tbl-colwidths="[20,36,34]"}

This order prevents the name or capability of an AI method from becoming a
substitute for a defined comparison and adequate evidence.

The red revision path in @fig-ontology-chain matters just as much as the path to
a decision. A failed simulation may reveal missing design information, while an
invalid candidate may expose a missing tool check. The architect must decide
whether to reject only that candidate or revise the scope. Either way, the
change and its effect on the claim should remain visible.

## The Design-Loop Card

@fig-structured-layer-above-rtl and @fig-ontology-chain give the full map. The
design-loop card gives a reader a short index to the same information in one
project or paper.

> **Design-loop card.** A design-loop card is a short guide to one architecture
> study. It points to the question, scope, and claim, then shows how architects
> and AI methods carried out the work. It also identifies evidence for and
> against the claim, stopping rules, the next decision supported by the evidence,
> and the person responsible for that decision. Detailed design state, evidence,
> and replay artifacts remain in linked records.

Authors can fill in the card as the work proceeds. Readers can use it to locate
missing information while the full evidence remains in linked artifacts.

In the public AlphaChip disagreement, the card would show which comparison and
evidence records a reviewer can locate and which are still missing. It would not
settle the technical claim.

::: {.callout-war-story title="The missing record became part of the disagreement"}
**The claim.** The AlphaChip paper reported a learned macro-placement method
that produced results comparable to or better than expert placement
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement].

**The gap.** Later researchers could not reconstruct every proprietary design,
training detail, and evaluation condition behind the strongest reported
results. The original authors argued that the reassessment had not run the
method as described
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL;
@GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed].

**The lesson.** A design-loop card would not settle that disagreement, but it
would show which baseline, scope, tool path, and evidence records were shared
and which were missing.
:::

For a separate, broader bounded study under the lighthouse prompt, one card
points to the claim, scope, tools, AI roles, and records that support the next
decision (@fig-design-loop-card-example). The underlying artifacts remain in
their source files.

![**A design-loop card indexes one architecture study.** It points readers to the study scope, claim, execution process, evidence, and decision owner. Detailed state, evidence, and replay artifacts remain in linked records.](images/F4b-design-loop-card-example){#fig-design-loop-card-example width="100%" fig-alt="A compact study index card for the lighthouse prompt, listing twelve fields from intent and task through failed runs, rejection checks and authority, what the evidence supports, and the accountable decision, under a claim banner, with a footer noting that the card indexes rather than replaces the study records."}

Read @fig-design-loop-card-example from the claim banner through the twelve
fields. The first fields identify the intent, task, and information available to
the AI method. The middle fields record its role, allowed actions, tool path,
results, and explanation. The final fields record failed runs, rejection checks,
what the evidence supports, and the accountable decision. A reader
can then see which detailed records to open and which connections are missing.

The card helps reviewers locate missing scope or evidence. A paper is hard to
compare when it reports a better search result but omits the cost of feedback,
rejected candidates, or limits of the environment. A tool is hard to assess
when it produces designs without stating which checks can rule them out. A
benchmark may measure model accuracy without testing architecture-relevant
reasoning. That can still be useful, but it does not by itself support an
architecture claim.

When the card omits the baseline, tool path, or supporting evidence, the author
should supply the missing information or narrow the claim. A complete card can
still describe an unsound design or unsupported claim, so completion does not
establish quality, novelty, insight, or correctness.

@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives the full twelve-field card, review
rubric, and machine-checkable schema. Detailed evidence and replay packages
remain linked artifacts rather than additional card fields. A validator can
check that the required fields and links are present, but it cannot establish
the quality of the architecture or its evidence.

## Open Research Questions

The research questions below ask what another architect needs to see, how much
workload and baseline information makes a result credible, and what reviewers
can catch before expensive tools are run.

1. **What does another architect need to see to review AI-assisted work?**
Reviewers need enough information to reconstruct the comparison, trace the AI
method's role, and see which next action the evidence could support. Authors may
still be unable to expose a proprietary toolchain or every internal artifact.
The open question is which fields materially help reviewers find missing
evidence, decisions that reach beyond the evidence, or steps that cannot be
examined. The design-loop card in @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card provides one
starting point rather than a settled minimum.

2. **What workload and baseline information must architects preserve so
reviewers can judge whether an AI-assisted result will hold elsewhere?**
Likely records include input distributions, benchmark construction, excluded
cases, holdout workloads, software versions, baseline assumptions, and
provenance. It remains unclear which combination exposes a conclusion that
fails on unseen workloads, under a changed baseline, or with higher-fidelity
feedback. AI methods and reviewers need enough information to distinguish a
useful architectural relationship from a result that holds only for one
evaluation setup.

3. **What should architects keep about alternatives rejected during
AI-assisted work?**
Rejected candidates show which parts of the design space were considered and
why they failed. A shared record might include invalid actions, failed timing
closures, proxy failures, repaired candidates, and alternatives rejected during
review. The unresolved question is how much negative evidence another
architect needs to determine which parts of the design space were actually
explored without retaining every intermediate artifact.

4. **What can reviewers catch before an AI method starts using expensive
tools?**
A reviewer or static checker can notice a missing baseline, invalid action
space, weak evidence rule, or planned next decision that the proposed checks
could not support. It cannot determine whether the architecture itself is good.
The open question is which early omissions reliably predict wasted tool runs or
an invalid comparison and which problems still require expert architectural
judgment.

## Conclusion

To use AI systematically, the architect first turns a broad goal into a specific
comparison, states what that comparison is meant to show, and records the
workload, baseline, allowed design choices, tools, evidence, and next decision.
Together, those elements form the bounded architecture study. Once they are
clear, AI methods can take on defined roles while the architect still owns the
scope and decision.

Architects already work from a question toward a decision. When AI participates,
the information it can use, the actions it may take, the feedback it receives,
and the decisions its results may support must be visible rather than assumed.
Another architect should be able to see what the AI system knew, what it
changed, how tools evaluated its work, and what the evidence supports.

The bounded study, architectural claim, and design loop serve different
purposes.

- The **bounded study** is the unit of work.
- The **architectural claim** is the testable statement another architect evaluates,
  together with its workload, baseline, design space, limits, and evidence.
- The **design loop** is the repeated execution and analysis used to carry out
  the work.

The work may end in better understanding, an artifact, a finding, or a decision.
The ontology, design-loop card, evidence records, and checks help another
architect review that outcome. They do not guarantee correctness or replace
architectural judgment. A successful rerun shows that the recorded inputs and
tool settings lead to the reported outputs; it does not show that the comparison
was the right one or that the evidence supports the decision.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Keep the three concepts distinct. The bounded study defines the work, the architectural claim states what another architect must evaluate, and the design loop is the execution and analysis process for iterative work.
- **Reader test:** Can you identify the architecture question, AI role,
  alternatives, experiments and tools, claim, results that challenge it,
  explanation, and next decision?
- **Up next:** @sec-data-representations-world-models asks what must be represented about a design, a proposed change, and its consequences so an AI method can act and another architect can trace the result.
:::
